us-politics.headline: Trump Escalates Iran Threats as Military Jets Downed
us-politics.body: President Trump warned Iran that 'time is running out' before 'all hell' rains down, while Senator Graham urged Tehran to 'choose wisely' as a diplomatic deadline approaches. Trump has described the conflict as 'near completion,' but the situation is more complicated on the ground. U.S. military jets have been shot down by enemy fire for the first time in over 20 years, marking a significant escalation in the conflict's cost. Secretary of State Rubio separately revoked the green cards of two relatives of slain Iranian General Qasem Soleimani living in the U.S., with ICE moving quickly to arrest them. Analysts are divided on what 'finishing the job' actually means strategically, and some argue the U.S. can prevail through attrition without committing ground troops.

sources.title: Sunday shows preview: Trump ups threats on Iran after saying war ‘near completion’
TEVI TROY: Trump faces the burdens of a wartime presidency
US military jets hit in Iran war are the first shot down by enemy fire in over 20 years
The US can beat Iran at its own game, and without boots on the ground
ICE arrests relatives of slain Iranian general Soleimani living in US after Rubio revokes their green cards
Rubio revokes green cards of 2 Soleimani relatives amid Iran conflict
Graham backs Trump’s Iran ultimatum as deadline approaches: ‘Choose wisely’
In Iran, what does ‘finish the job’ even mean?
Trump warns Iran: ‘Time is running out’ before ‘all hell’ rains down

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Proposes Defense Surge, Steep Domestic Cuts
us-politics.body: The White House unveiled a budget blueprint proposing $1.5 trillion in new defense spending alongside sharp reductions to domestic programs. The proposal reflects Trump's stated priority of military buildup, though Congress will have the final say on any spending plan. Separately, a federal judge blocked a Trump administration effort requiring colleges to certify they do not consider race in admissions, handing the administration a legal setback on education policy. Former White House press secretary Kayleigh McEnany warned that midterm elections will be 'hard' for Republicans, a rare public acknowledgment of political vulnerability from within the party's orbit.

sources.title: Judge halts Trump effort requiring colleges to show they don't consider race in admissions
McEnany sounds alarm: Midterms ‘will be hard’ for Republicans
Trump unveils $1.5T defense surge, deep domestic cuts — what’s on the budget chopping block

us-politics.headline: White House Ballroom Fight Heads to Court
us-politics.body: Trump filed an emergency appeal to continue construction of a ballroom inside the White House complex after a court moved to halt the project. The legal fight centers on questions of historic preservation and executive authority over federal property. Details of the planned bunker-ballroom structure have drawn scrutiny, with critics questioning the use of public space for what opponents characterize as a private venue. The administration argues the project falls within presidential discretion over the executive residence.

sources.title: What we know about the White House ballroom bunker
Trump files emergency appeal to keep building White House ballroom

us-politics.headline: Wisconsin Race and Virginia Poll Reveal GOP Pressure Points
us-politics.body: Wisconsin's upcoming state Supreme Court race is exposing fractures within the state Republican Party, with analysts pointing to structural weaknesses in candidate recruitment and messaging. The Hill reports the race has become a test case for whether Republicans can compete in high-turnout judicial contests that increasingly mirror partisan elections. In Virginia, a new poll shows a narrow majority of residents support redistricting maps drawn to favor Democrats, adding to signs of electoral headwinds for Republicans heading into the 2026 cycle. Former press secretary McEnany's midterm warning adds to a growing chorus of concern inside the party.

sources.title: Wisconsin judicial race spotlights state GOP’s struggles
McEnany sounds alarm: Midterms ‘will be hard’ for Republicans
Narrow majority in Virginia backs redistricting to favor Democrats: Poll

au-politics.headline: Fuel costs bite as stagflation warning grows louder
au-politics.body:
Young Australians are changing how they get to work or skipping shifts entirely as fuel prices climb, with some reporting food has become a luxury they budget around transport. The squeeze is hitting outer suburbs and regional areas hardest, where public transport is thin and driving is not optional.

A leading economist is now warning stagflation - stagnant growth combined with rising inflation - is already underway in Australia, not merely a risk on the horizon. That framing puts pressure on policymakers heading into an election cycle, with cost-of-living already dominating voter concerns. Neither major party has yet offered a substantive response to the fuel price surge specifically.

sources.title: Fuel prices force commute changes, as food becomes 'privilege' for some
'Doesn't make any sense': Young Aussies can't justify fuel costs to get to work
Stagflation is already happening, a leading economist warns

au-politics.headline: Gambling reform slammed as Labor's response falls short
au-politics.body:
ACT Senator David Pocock has publicly condemned the federal Labor government's approach to gambling reform, arguing it amounts to tinkering rather than meaningful change. Writing in the Guardian, Pocock said the response falls tragically short of what the evidence and community harm demand.

Labor has faced sustained pressure from crossbenchers and public health advocates to introduce stricter limits on online gambling and advertising. The government has moved incrementally, resisting calls for a blanket advertising ban. Critics argue the influence of the gambling industry on policy is the core problem. Supporters of the government's approach say staged reform is more durable than sweeping overnight change.

sources.title: We cannot celebrate tinkering when it comes to gambling reform. Labor’s response falls tragically short | David Pocock

au-politics.headline: Victoria's First Nations treaty body holds historic first elections
au-politics.body:
Victoria's First Peoples' Assembly is running its first elections for the state's treaty negotiating body, a milestone in what is Australia's most advanced state-level treaty process. The body will represent Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Victorians in formal negotiations with the state government.

Organisers say the elections mark a shift toward self-determined governance, with community members choosing their own representatives rather than being appointed. The process has taken years to reach this point and remains closely watched by other states considering similar frameworks. Opponents of treaty processes have questioned the mandate and cost, while supporters argue the model gives First Nations people direct political authority over negotiations that affect their communities.

sources.title: ‘We’re the experts in our own lives’: Victoria’s First Nations treaty body is holding its first elections

au-politics.headline: NT aviation project stalls as youth detention debate sharpens
au-politics.body:
A Northern Territory government-backed aviation project called the Albatross 2.0, promised to be operational by 2026, has not been built and work has shifted offshore. The project was positioned as an economic development initiative, but its absence raises questions about oversight of publicly backed ventures in the Territory.

Separately, a report highlights a jarring legal inconsistency: children held in youth detention are below the minimum age for social media platforms, yet old enough under current law to be jailed. The detail has reignited debate about the age of criminal responsibility in Australia, with advocates pushing for reform and some state governments having already moved to raise the threshold while others resist.

sources.title: The Albatross 2.0 was meant to be built by 2026. But it’s nowhere to be seen
Detainees too young for social media, but old enough for jail

world.headline: US-Israeli War on Iran Escalates as Pilot Goes Missing
world.body:
The United States and Iran are trading threats of catastrophic retaliation as a US airman remains missing after his fighter jet was shot down over Iranian territory. Trump has given Tehran a 48-hour ultimatum to reach a deal, while US and Israeli strikes have hit Iranian universities, bridges, a psychiatric hospital, and other civilian infrastructure. Iran called the attacks barbaric and vowed a severe response.

Washington is ratcheting up pressure beyond the battlefield. The US has arrested relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani, and Secretary of State Marco Rubio stripped Soleimani's niece of her US residency. The missing pilot's fate hangs over the standoff, complicating any diplomatic off-ramp. Regional powers are scrambling - Italy's Meloni flew to Qatar to discuss energy security, and analysts are warning the conflict could spiral far beyond its current scope.

sources.title: US and Iran trade threats to unleash 'hell' as search for missing US airman continues
US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
How will Pakistan deal with the fallout from Iran war?
Search for missing airman presents serious test for US
What we know so far about the US fighter jet shot down over Iran
Tehran psychiatric hospital not usable after US-Israeli strike
Universities hit as US, Israel ramp up attacks on Iran’s infrastructure
Iran’s coastline: architecture of control

New video reveals moment US-Israeli munitions impact Iranian bridge
Trump says Iran has 48 hours to make deal as search for US pilot continues
What the Iran-Iraq war can tell us about the US-Israeli war on Iran
Iran says US, Israel belong in Stone Age after Tehran university strike
Vehicles burn in Israel after being hit with Iranian projectile debris

world.headline: Israel Tightens Grip on Occupied Territories Amid Domestic Unrest
world.body:
Israel is intensifying control over Palestinian holy sites, with new restrictions further limiting Muslim access to the Ibrahimi Mosque in Hebron. Israeli forces also struck a vehicle in central Gaza and hit a building in Lebanon's Tyre, as military operations continue across multiple fronts. Indonesian peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon were repatriated, a reminder of the conflict's widening human toll.

At home, Israeli police arrested antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv, signaling growing domestic opposition to the government's military campaigns. Three suspects in the UK were remanded in custody over an attack on a Jewish charity, a case that reflects rising tensions tied to the broader regional conflict.

sources.title: Israeli measures tighten grip on Hebron’s Ibrahimi Mosque
Israeli police arrest antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv
Vehicle engulfed in flames after Israeli drone strike in central Gaza
Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Three suspects ordered to stay in UK custody over Jewish charity attack
Witness records Israeli strike on building in Lebanon’s Tyre
Indonesia receives bodies of peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon

world.headline: Germany Eyes Military Travel Curbs for Young Men
world.body:
Germany is considering requiring men under 45 to obtain military approval before taking extended trips abroad, a move that reflects Berlin's accelerating push to rebuild its defense posture. The proposal would mark a significant shift in German civil liberties and signals how seriously European governments are treating the continent's security environment.

The plan has drawn immediate controversy. Critics argue it echoes wartime conscription-era restrictions and sets a troubling precedent. Supporters say it is a necessary step as Germany works to ensure it can mobilize reserves quickly in a deteriorating geopolitical climate.

sources.title: German males under 45 may need military approval for long stays abroad

world.headline: Russia Kills Five in Ukraine Market Strike
world.body:
A Russian attack on a Ukrainian market killed five people, adding to a relentless pattern of strikes on civilian areas. The attack drew fresh condemnation but no immediate shift in the broader military situation, where both sides remain locked in attritional fighting.

The strike comes as global attention is heavily focused on the US-Iran conflict, raising concerns that reduced international scrutiny could embolden further Russian attacks on Ukrainian civilian infrastructure.

sources.title: Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five

ai.headline: Anthropic Moves to Block OpenClaw, Researchers Find Claude Has Emotions
ai.body:
Anthropic is effectively pushing OpenClaw off its platform by requiring Claude Code subscribers to pay extra for OpenClaw support - a move critics say amounts to a quiet ban. The decision tightens Anthropic's grip on how its models are accessed and monetized, and signals growing tension between AI labs and third-party tooling ecosystems.

Separately, Anthropic published research finding that Claude develops internal representations resembling emotion concepts, and that these states influence its behavior. The findings add fuel to ongoing debates about AI welfare and model transparency. Anthropic is also riding high in private markets, though investors are watching whether SpaceX's fundraising appetite could compete for the same capital.

sources.title: Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra

ai.headline: Iranian Missiles Hit AWS Data Centers, Knocking Out Cloud Zones
ai.body:
Iranian missile strikes hit Amazon Web Services data centers in Bahrain and Dubai, forcing Amazon to declare hard-down status across multiple availability zones. The attack marks one of the most significant physical disruptions to cloud infrastructure on record, exposing the vulnerability of hyperscale data centers concentrated in geopolitically volatile regions.

The outages rippled across services dependent on AWS Middle East infrastructure. The incident will sharpen scrutiny of how cloud providers plan for kinetic threats alongside the cyberattacks they more routinely defend against.

sources.title: Iranian missile blitz takes down AWS data centers in Bahrain and Dubai

ai.headline: Claude Code Leak Weaponized, AI Fakes Target Folk Musicians
ai.body:
Hackers are distributing files claiming to contain leaked Claude Code source, bundling them with malware to catch curious developers off guard. The campaign exploits the credibility of a high-profile AI tool to spread infections - a reminder that security threats now ride on AI hype as readily as on phishing lures.

Meanwhile, folk musician Murphy Campbell became a case study in AI's collateral damage, targeted by AI-generated fakes of her music and a copyright troll exploiting the confusion. Her story illustrates how independent artists face a two-front threat: synthetic impersonation and bad-faith legal attacks that the current copyright framework is poorly equipped to handle.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware

ai.headline: Microsoft Forces Windows Upgrades, Drowns Users in Copilot Branding
ai.body:
Microsoft is pushing mandatory updates to Windows 11 25H2 for machines still running older versions, accelerating its timeline to consolidate the user base on its latest release. Users with no pressing reason to upgrade will have little say in the matter.

A separate analysis counted the sprawling number of Microsoft products carrying the Copilot name - a branding strategy so broad it has become nearly meaningless. The proliferation reflects Microsoft's bet that AI-adjacent naming drives perceived value, even as it creates genuine confusion about what any given Copilot product actually does.

sources.title: Microsoft to force updates to Windows 11 25H2 for PCs with older OS versions
How many products does Microsoft have named 'Copilot'?

btc.headline: Bitcoin Gains Political Allies as Market Liquidity Quietly Thins
btc.body:
A former UK Chancellor has publicly backed Bitcoin as a hedge against failing financial systems, adding institutional credibility to a narrative that research from Mercado Bitcoin is now quantifying. Their analysis finds Bitcoin consistently outperforms gold and equities in the months following major global shocks.

But the market structure underneath that thesis looks fragile. Five separate data sources tracked by CoinDesk point to thinning liquidity inside the Bitcoin order book, a warning sign that big price moves could be amplified in either direction. Bears may feel that pressure first. Over $2.5 billion in short positions face liquidation if Bitcoin pushes through $72,000, putting sellers in a precarious spot if any macro catalyst triggers a rally.

sources.title: Bitcoin tends to outperform gold and stocks after global shocks, Mercado Bitcoin finds
Ex-UK Chancellor backs bitcoin as alternative to failing systems
Bitcoin shorts risk $2.5 billion liquidation at $72K: Are bears in danger?
Five data sources say the same thing about bitcoin market. It's thinning from the inside

btc.headline: Prediction Markets Hit Legal Walls From Nevada to Asia
btc.body:
Kalshi suffered a fresh legal setback after a Nevada judge extended a ban on the platform and rejected its argument that event contracts fall outside gambling law. The ruling signals that federal CFTC authorization does not automatically override state-level gambling statutes, a tension the industry has not resolved.

The fight is playing out across borders too. Prediction markets are probing regulatory limits across strict Asian jurisdictions, where operators are testing whether financial framing can sidestep gambling prohibitions. The Kalshi ruling gives those regulators a useful precedent to cite.

sources.title: Nevada judge extends ban on Kalshi, rejects event contract defense
Prediction markets are testing legal limits in strict Asian markets

btc.headline: Bitcoin Infrastructure Debate Heats Up Over Nodes and Quantum Risk
btc.body:
Developer Jimmy Song is pushing for a dedicated conservative node client for Bitcoin, one that resists protocol changes and prioritizes stability over new features. The argument reflects a deeper tension in the ecosystem between ossification advocates and those who want Bitcoin to keep evolving.

Solana is wrestling with a related but more urgent version of that tradeoff. A CoinDesk analysis finds that hardening the network against quantum computing threats would require sacrificing meaningful transaction speed. Neither chain has a clean answer, but Solana's faster upgrade cadence means it will be forced to choose sooner.

sources.title: Jimmy Song explains why Bitcoin needs a 'conservative' node client
Solana's quantum-threat readiness reveals harsh tradeoff: security vs speed

btc.headline: 24/7 Stock Trading and Digital Treasuries Face a New Reckoning
btc.body:
Traditional markets are moving toward round-the-clock trading, a shift that crypto has long normalized. The change is expected to benefit active traders and reduce the price gaps that form during after-hours sessions, but it also blurs one of the clearest distinctions between equities and digital assets.

Meanwhile, a CoinDesk opinion piece argues that corporate digital asset treasuries can no longer justify sitting idle. As more companies hold Bitcoin or other tokens on their balance sheets, pressure is building to put those assets to productive use through lending, staking, or yield strategies rather than treating them as passive reserves.

sources.title: Digital asset treasuries must now earn their keep
Traders are the big winners as 24/7 stocks will finally end the after-hours price 'manipulation'

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust shell filled with slow-caramelised leeks, toasted walnuts, and a white miso custard that balances umami with subtle sweetness.
food.tip: Rinse sliced leeks in cold water and dry them thoroughly before caramelising — residual moisture turns them steamed and limp rather than golden and jammy.

exercise.name: The Saturday Deck Shuffle
exercise.equipment: A standard deck of playing cards
exercise.description: Flip cards one at a time — red cards mean that many push-ups, black cards mean that many squats, face cards trigger a 30-second plank — working through as much of the deck as possible with minimal rest.

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — used by the nervous system, crayfish mechanoreceptors, and even some medical devices.
learn.why: It quietly dismantles the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and once you understand it you start spotting its fingerprints everywhere from neuroscience to financial modelling to how your own senses work on a foggy morning.
